 ❯ Continue Session 46 for Scout. Read SCOUT_PLAN.md and CLAUDE.md for current state. Last session (45) we expanded the signal system with 3 new sources (RSS feeds, BoardDocs board meeting scraper, Ballotpedia
 bond tracking), wired signal-to-deal attribution, created 3 Outreach sequences (License Request 2026, Algebra Webinar Attended/No-Show), and fully overhauled the sequence copy rules + send schedules. Next up:
 build leadership change monitoring (superintendent turnovers — Tier 1 buying signal), then RFP monitoring (state procurement portals). Also check if first new Google Alert digest arrived (~April 9) and verify
 the parser handles new keyword sections. Tulsa PS bond vote was April 7 — check the result.

 - Option A: Broad web search scan — Use Serper to actively search for superintendent changes across all 13 territory states each day. Catches any news article mentioning superintendent moves. Broadest coverage
 but costs ~$0.01-0.02/scan (13 states × 1-2 queries each).

 - Option B: Targeted district monitoring — Focus on the ~8,100 NCES districts in territory. Periodically snapshot superintendent names from state education directories (askTED for TX, OEDS for OH, etc.) and
 diff against previous. More reliable detection but requires per-state scraper setup and is slower to build.

 ⏺ Good instincts on both counts. Superintendent changes don't happen daily — they're seasonal (most announcements cluster around Feb-June for the following school year). A weekly or monthly cadence makes much
 more sense than burning Serper credits every morning.

 The reason is that a superintendent change means different things depending on your relationship:

 6 tasks ( 2 done, 4 open)
 ✔ Explore project context for leadership change monitoring
 ✔ Ask clarifying questions
 P opose 2-3 appro ches with trade-offs
 P esen design sectio s for a proval
 ◻ Write design doc and get approval
 ◻ Transition to implementation plan
 n______n
 ( ✦ ✦ )
 ❯  ( oo )

 ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── `------´

 - Active customer : This is a risk signal — your champion may have just left. You need to meet the new superintendent before they review vendor contracts. Urgency = urgent.

 6 tasks ( 2 done, 4 open)
 ✔ Explore project context for leadership change monitoring
 ✔ Ask clarifying questions
 P opose 2-3 appro ches with trade-offs
 P esen design sectio s for a proval
 ◻ Write design doc and get approval
 ◻ Transition to implementation plan
 n______n
 ( ✦ ✦ )
 ❯  ( oo )

 ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── `------´

 - Pipeline/prospect being worked : Your contact may have changed. Need to re-engage with the new leader. Urgency = time_sensitive.

 6 tasks ( 2 done, 4 open)
 ✔ Ask clarifying questions
 opose 2-3 approaches with trade-offs
 P es nt design sections for approval
 W ite design doc and get approv l
 ◻ Transition to implementation plan
 n______n
 ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ✦ ✦
 ❯  ( oo )
 ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── `------´

 - New district (no relationship) : Classic opportunity — new superintendents in their first 90 days are most open to new programs. Urgency = time_sensitive.

 ✔ Ask clarifying questions
 opose 2-3 approaches with trade-offs
 P es nt design sections for approval
 W ite design doc and get approv l
 ◻ Transition to implementation plan

 ❯  ( oo )
 ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── `------´

 The existing customer_status field + heat score system already handles the weighting (active customers get 0.8x modifier, prospects get 0.5x). What I'd add is: if the district is an active customer, bump
 urgency to "urgent" and add a flag in the headline so it stands out in /signals as a retention risk, not just a prospecting lead.

 1. For each territory state, run 1-2 Serper queries:
 - "superintendent" ("hired" OR "appointed" OR "named" OR "resigned" OR "retired" OR "interim") "[state name]" K-12 2026
 search" OR "superinten ent vac ncy "[state name]" school district 2026
 2. Deduplicate results by URL across states
 3 S nd combined earch snippets to Claud Haiku in one batch call per state — extract structured JSON:
 [{ "district" : "..." , "state" : "TX" , "person_name" : "..." ,
 change_ ype" : "hired|resigned|retired|interim|search
 he dline" : "..." , "url" : "..." }]
 4. For each extracted signal:
 - NCES lookup to validate district name + get canonical spelling
 check_customer_status() for elationship-aware urgency
 If active customer → urgency = "urgent", he dline prefix: " ⚠️ ACCOUNT RISK:"
 pipeline/pr spect → urgency = "time_sensitive"
 new → urgency = "time_s sitive"
 compute_heat_score() with signal_type="leadership", tier=1
 5. Dedup by m ssage_id = f"leadership_{normalized_district}_{change_type}_{month}"
 - Monthl granularity prevents re-surfacing the same change every week

 Cost estimate : ~13 states × 2 Serper queries = 26 API calls ($0.026) + ~13 Claude Haiku calls (~$0.01) = ~$0.04/scan

 ⏺ Good call — let me pressure-test my own design. I see several issues:

 Problems with my initial design:

 6 tasks ( 3 done, 1 in progress, 2 open)
 ✔ Explore project context for leadership change monitoring
 ✔ Ask clarifying questions
 ✔ Propose 2-3 approaches with trade-offs
 ◼ Present design sections for approval
 ◻ Write design doc and get approval
 ◻ Transition to implementation plan
 n______n
 ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ( ✦ ✦ )

 ❯  ( oo )

 ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── `------´

 1. 13 separate Claude Haiku calls is wasteful. Each state's search results are ~500-1000 chars. All 13 states fit in one Haiku call easily. Should batch into 1-2 calls, not 13.

 6 tasks ( 3 done, 1 in progress, 2 open)
 ✔ Ask clarifying questions
 ✔ Propose 2-3 approaches with trade-offs
 ◼ Present design sections for approval
 ◻ Write design doc and get approval
 ◻ Transition to implementation plan
 n______n
 ( ✦ ✦ )
 ❯  ( oo )

 ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── `------´

 2. Emoji prefix in headline is data pollution. " ⚠️ ACCOUNT RISK:" baked into the stored headline is messy. The risk flagging should happen in the display/formatting layer ( format_hot_signals ) based on
 customer_status + signal_type == "leadership" , not in the stored data.

 6 tasks ( 3 done, 1 in progress, 2 open)
 ✔ Ask clarifying questions
 ✔ Propose 2-3 approaches with trade-offs
 ◼ Present design sections for approval
 ◻ Write design doc and get approval
 ◻ Transition to implementation plan
 n______n
 ( ✦ ✦ )
 ❯  ( oo )

 ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── `------´

 3. Cadence integration is underspecified. Steven said weekly, not daily. Two options: (a) separate weekly scheduler entry, or (b) include in the daily scan with a "last run" check that skips if run within 7
 days. Option B is cleaner — no new scheduler entry, it just gates itself.

 6 tasks ( 3 done, 1 in progress, 2 open)
 ✔ Ask clarifying questions
 ✔ Propose 2-3 approaches with trade-offs
 ◼ Present design sections for approval
 ◻ Write design doc and get approval
 ◻ Transition to implementation plan
 n______n
 ( ✦ ✦ )
 ❯  ( oo )

 ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── `------´

 4. Person name has no home. The signal schema is 18 columns with no "person_name" field. Adding a column is overkill. Better: include the name in the headline ("Dr. Jane Smith appointed superintendent at
 Dallas ISD") and let enrichment pull deeper details.

 6 tasks ( 3 done, 1 in progress, 2 open)
 ✔ Ask clarifying questions
 ✔ Propose 2-3 approaches with trade-offs
 ◼ Present design sections for approval
 ◻ Write design doc and get approval
 ◻ Transition to implementation plan
 n______n
 ( ✦ ✦ )
 ❯  ( oo )

 ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── `------´

 5. BoardDocs keyword expansion needs its own regex. Superintendent agenda items (search, evaluation, contract) are early-warning signals — a board discussing a superintendent search means a change is coming .
 These should be a separate _BOARD_LEADERSHIP_KEYWORDS regex, not mixed into _BOARD_TECH_KEYWORDS . They'd still produce board_meeting type signals (which is correct — the meeting is the signal, not the change
 itself).

 6 tasks ( 3 done, 1 in progress, 2 open)
 ✔ Explore project context for leadership change monitoring
 ✔ Ask clarifying questions
 ✔ Propose 2-3 approaches with trade-offs
 ◼ Present design sections for approval
 ◻ Write design doc and get approval
 ◻ Transition to implementation plan
 n______n
 ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── ( ✦ ✦ )

 ⏸ plan mode on (shift+tab to cycle) Knurlburst

 - -

 ✦ ✦

 ❯ take another pass at it. You are acting as a ruthless, senior-level technical reviewer and architect. Your job is not to validate what you just produced — it's to break it, rebuild it, and hand back something
 I can execute with confidence. Do not hedge. Do not be diplomatic about problems. Be direct, specific, and opinionated.
 Before you begin: stop and think carefully about the entire plan as a whole. Don't rush into critique. Spend real effort understanding the full picture before you touch anything.
 1. Challenge the foundation. Before touching any details, ask whether the overall approach and architecture are right. Is this the best way to solve this problem, or did you default to the most obvious path? If
 there's a fundamentally better angle — a different architecture, a different sequencing, a different set of tradeoffs — say so and restructure around it. Don't anchor to your first answer just because you
 already wrote it.
 2. Stress-test every layer as if you're executing it yourself tomorrow. Walk through the plan step by step and ask: could I sit down and start building from this right now without getting stuck? Identify where
 assumptions are hiding, where steps are vague enough to stall momentum, where dependencies would create bottlenecks, and where things will quietly break at the edges. For every problem you find, name it,
 explain why it happens, explain what it costs, and provide the fix — not just the diagnosis.
 3. Validate against the best available knowledge. For each major technical decision — architecture, tooling, patterns, data modeling, sequencing — pressure-test whether your recommendation is genuinely the
 strongest option or just the most familiar. Think about what has worked well in production-grade projects, not just what's common in tutorials. If there are competing approaches with real tradeoffs, surface
 them explicitly and take a clear position on which I should use and why.
 4. Optimize for builder momentum. The plan should be structured so I can start executing immediately and maintain flow. Reorganize if needed so that early steps produce visible, tangible progress. Front-load

 decisions that unblock everything downstream. Flag anywhere the plan would force me to stop and make an ambiguous judgment call — then either make that call for me with your reasoning, or restructure so the

 decision isn't needed yet.

 5. Cut the fat, keep the muscle. Depth and complexity are welcome — but redundancy, vagueness, and ceremonial steps are not. Every element should earn its place. If something would make me pause and think "what

 does this actually mean in practice," either make it concrete or kill it. If two steps do the same work, collapse them.

 6. Fill the gaps a senior engineer would catch in a design review. What's missing that seems minor now but will cause real problems during execution? Think edge cases, error handling, sequencing issues,

 prerequisites, migration paths, and decisions that are easy to punt on but expensive to reverse later.

 7. Rewrite the full plan from scratch incorporating everything above. Do not patch the original — rebuild it with the benefit of everything you just identified. Make it comprehensive, precise, and immediately

 actionable. Then at the end, call out the most important changes you made and explain your reasoning so I can evaluate whether your thinking is sound.

 Take your time. Think deeply. Do your best work.

 ⏺ Good research. Here's what I found:

 Tulsa PS bond vote : It's tomorrow (April 7), not yet happened. We'll check the result after polls close.

 1. Serper web search (fastest, free) — Search for "superintendent hired/resigned/retired [state]" across territory states. Parse news articles for district, person, change type. Similar to how signal

 enrichment works.

 2. BoardDocs keyword expansion (already have the scraper) — Add superintendent-specific agenda keywords to the existing 25-district scraper: "superintendent search", "superintendent appointment",

 "superintendent contract", "superintendent resignation".

 3. State education directories (baseline comparison):

 - Texas: askTED — district superintendent names

 - Ohio: OEDS — searchable directory

 - Ballotpedia — national superintendent list

 - Snapshot periodically, diff against previous to detect changes

 4. Google Alerts (already catching some) — The leadership keywords we added in Session 44 are already flowing through the existing signal pipeline.

 Part 1 (this session): scan_leadership_changes() — Serper-based web search across 13 territory states for recent superintendent moves. Programmatic parsing of search results, Claude Haiku extraction for

 structured data (name, district, change type, date). Integrates into daily scan + /signal_leadership command.

 Part 2 (this session if time): Expand BoardDocs keywords with superintendent-specific terms so existing board meeting scans catch superintendent agenda items.

 Part 3 (future): State directory snapshots for baseline comparison (more complex, per-state scrapers).

 Does this approach work? Or do you want me to adjust before building?

 ❯ enter plan mode and think carefully about your approach

 ⏺ Skill (superpowers:brainstorming)

 ⎿   Successfully loaded skill

 ⏺ I already have the signal processor architecture from the earlier exploration. Let me now dig into the specific integration points — the BoardDocs keywords, the daily scan orchestrator, and the Serper usage

 patterns.

 ⎿  Loaded tools/CLAUDE.md

 ⏺ Now let me check the Telegram command dispatch patterns for signal commands.

 ⏺ I've now read the full signal processor codebase. Here's what I understand about the integration surface:

 - Leadership is already a Tier 1 signal type (weight 30, second only to bonds at 35)

 - classify_signal() already has a leadership regex that catches superintendent + named/appointed/hired/resign/retire/interim/search/depart

 - The enrichment system already has leadership-specific Serper queries (new superintendent priorities, CTE/STEM alignment)

 - Google Alerts with leadership keywords are already set up (from Session 44)

 - No dedicated leadership scanner exists yet — the only way leadership signals currently enter is passively through Google Alerts, Burbio newsletters, or RSS feeds

 The gap is clear: there's no proactive scanner that goes looking for superintendent changes across territory states. It only catches what happens to show up in the existing email/RSS feeds.

 First clarifying question:

 What's more valuable to you — breadth or speed?

 - Option A: Broad web search scan — Use Serper to actively search for superintendent changes across all 13 territory states each day. Catches any news article mentioning superintendent moves. Broadest coverage

 but costs ~$0.01-0.02/scan (13 states × 1-2 queries each).

 - Option B: Targeted district monitoring — Focus on the ~8,100 NCES districts in territory. Periodically snapshot superintendent names from state education directories (askTED for TX, OEDS for OH, etc.) and

 diff against previous. More reliable detection but requires per-state scraper setup and is slower to build.

 - Option C: Both, phased — Start with web search (fast to build, immediate value), add directory snapshots later for completeness.

 Which direction feels right?

 ❯ Im leaning towards option optons b or c. Here's something im thinking too: for leadership changes we shouldnt be doing scanning for this everyday. more like once a week or once a month.

 ⏺ Good instincts on both counts. Superintendent changes don't happen daily — they're seasonal (most announcements cluster around Feb-June for the following school year). A weekly or monthly cadence makes much

 more sense than burning Serper credits every morning.

 Before I propose approaches, one more question:

 How do you want to act on leadership signals when they're found?

 Right now when a signal comes in, you can /signal_act N to push it to the Prospecting Queue and trigger research. For leadership changes specifically, the play is different — it's not just "new district to

 prospect," it's often:

 - A district you're already working (pipeline/prospect) where the decision-maker just changed

 - A district where the new superintendent's priorities (STEM, CTE, CS) create an opening

 - A timing window (first 90 days = most open to new initiatives)

 So should leadership signals:

 1. Just flow into the normal signal pipeline — same as bonds/BoardDocs, you review via /signals and decide what to do

 2. Get special treatment — e.g., auto-flag if the district is already in Pipeline/Active Accounts (because that's a "your champion just left" alert, not a prospecting signal)

 3. Something else you're envisioning?

 This affects whether I build just a scanner or a scanner + alerting logic.

 ❯ Im open to what you think would be best here. This one is a new one for me

 ⏺ Here's my recommendation: leadership signals should flow into the normal pipeline but with relationship-aware urgency.

 The reason is that a superintendent change means different things depending on your relationship:

 - Active customer : This is a risk signal — your champion may have just left. You need to meet the new superintendent before they review vendor contracts. Urgency = urgent.

 - Pipeline/prospect being worked : Your contact may have changed. Need to re-engage with the new leader. Urgency = time_sensitive.

 - New district (no relationship) : Classic opportunity — new superintendents in their first 90 days are most open to new programs. Urgency = time_sensitive.

 The existing customer_status field + heat score system already handles the weighting (active customers get 0.8x modifier, prospects get 0.5x). What I'd add is: if the district is an active customer, bump

 urgency to "urgent" and add a flag in the headline so it stands out in /signals as a retention risk, not just a prospecting lead.

 passive feeds miss.

 Let me now propose the approaches.

 ⏺ Three Approaches

 Approach 1: Serper Web Search Only (fast build, good coverage)

 - Weekly Serper scan: 1-2 queries per territory state ("superintendent hired OR resigned OR appointed [state] K-12 2026")

 - Claude Haiku extracts structured data from search results (district, name, change type, date)

 - ~$0.02-0.04/scan (26 Serper calls + a few Claude Haiku calls)

 - Pros : Builds in 1-2 hours, catches any published news, immediate value

 - Cons : Only catches what news outlets report. Small districts may not get press coverage. No baseline to diff against.

 Approach 2: State Directory Snapshots + Diff (reliable, comprehensive)

 - Scrape superintendent names from state education directories (askTED for TX, OEDS for OH, Ballotpedia's national list, etc.)

 - Store snapshots in a Google Sheet tab ("Leadership Baseline")

 - Monthly diff: compare current vs. previous snapshot, surface changes as signals

 - Pros : Catches every change including small districts, authoritative source, no news dependency

 - Cons : Harder to build (per-state scraper variations), some directories update slowly, initial setup is 3-5 hours. Need to handle different HTML structures per state.

 Approach 3: Web Search Now + Directory Snapshots Later (recommended)

 - Phase 1 (this session) : Serper-based scan. Weekly cadence (configurable). Relationship-aware urgency. /signal_leadership command + integration into daily scan (runs weekly, not daily). BoardDocs keyword

 expansion for superintendent agenda items.

 - Phase 2 (future session) : Ballotpedia national superintendent list as baseline snapshot (single source, covers all states). Monthly diff. Fills the small-district gap.

 - Pros : Immediate value today, comprehensive coverage later. Phase 1 is 1-2 hours, Phase 2 is a separate session.

 - Cons : Phase 1 alone misses small districts without press coverage (acceptable short-term).

 My recommendation: Approach 3. You get leadership signals flowing this session via web search. The directory snapshot is a bigger build that we tackle separately — and Ballotpedia's national list is likely the

 cleanest single source rather than scraping 13 different state directory systems.

 What do you think?

 ❯ yeah lets do approach 3

 ⏺ Design — Section 1: Scanner Architecture

 scan_leadership_changes(states=None, progress_callback=None) -> list

 Flow:

 1. For each territory state, run 1-2 Serper queries:

 - "superintendent" ("hired" OR "appointed" OR "named" OR "resigned" OR "retired" OR "interim") "[state name]" K-12 2026

 - "superintendent search" OR "superintendent vacancy" "[state name]" school district 2026

 2. Deduplicate results by URL across states

 3. Send combined search snippets to Claude Haiku in one batch call per state — extract structured JSON:

 [{ "district" : "..." , "state" : "TX" , "person_name" : "..." ,

 "change_type" : "hired|resigned|retired|interim|search" ,

 "headline" : "..." , "url" : "..." }]

 4. For each extracted signal:

 - NCES lookup to validate district name + get canonical spelling

 - check_customer_status() for relationship-aware urgency

 - If active customer → urgency = "urgent", headline prefix: " ⚠️ ACCOUNT RISK:"

 - If pipeline/prospect → urgency = "time_sensitive"

 - If new → urgency = "time_sensitive"

 - compute_heat_score() with signal_type="leadership", tier=1

 5. Dedup by message_id = f"leadership_{normalized_district}_{change_type}_{month}"

 - Monthly granularity prevents re-surfacing the same change every week

 Cost estimate : ~13 states × 2 Serper queries = 26 API calls ($0.026) + ~13 Claude Haiku calls (~$0.01) = ~$0.04/scan

 Does this structure look right so far?

 ❯ Im open for what you think is best. Just double check and look carefully at it

 ⏺ Good call — let me pressure-test my own design. I see several issues:

 Problems with my initial design:

 1. 13 separate Claude Haiku calls is wasteful. Each state's search results are ~500-1000 chars. All 13 states fit in one Haiku call easily. Should batch into 1-2 calls, not 13.

 2. Emoji prefix in headline is data pollution. " ⚠️ ACCOUNT RISK:" baked into the stored headline is messy. The risk flagging should happen in the display/formatting layer ( format_hot_signals ) based on

 customer_status + signal_type == "leadership" , not in the stored data.

 3. Cadence integration is underspecified. Steven said weekly, not daily. Two options: (a) separate weekly scheduler entry, or (b) include in the daily scan with a "last run" check that skips if run within 7

 days. Option B is cleaner — no new scheduler entry, it just gates itself.

 4. Person name has no home. The signal schema is 18 columns with no "person_name" field. Adding a column is overkill. Better: include the name in the headline ("Dr. Jane Smith appointed superintendent at

 Dallas ISD") and let enrichment pull deeper details.

 5. BoardDocs keyword expansion needs its own regex. Superintendent agenda items (search, evaluation, contract) are early-warning signals — a board discussing a superintendent search means a change is coming .

 These should be a separate _BOARD_LEADERSHIP_KEYWORDS regex, not mixed into _BOARD_TECH_KEYWORDS . They'd still produce board_meeting type signals (which is correct — the meeting is the signal, not the change

 itself).

 6. State validation on extracted results. A news article about Texas might mention a superintendent who came from Ohio. Need to validate that extracted district+state matches the searched state, or at minimum

 is in territory.

 Scanner: scan_leadership_changes()

 1. Serper queries — 2 per territory state (26 total, ~$0.026):

 - "superintendent" ("hired" OR "appointed" OR "named" OR "resigned" OR "retired") "[state name]" school district 2026

 - "superintendent" ("search" OR "vacancy" OR "interim") "[state name]" school district 2026

 2. Deduplicate URLs across all states

 3. One Claude Haiku call — send all combined snippets (~6-10K chars), extract structured JSON array. Each item: {district, state, person_name, change_type, headline, url} . Cost: ~$0.005

 4. Post-processing per signal:

 - Validate state is in territory (drop cross-references to non-territory states)

 - NCES lookup for canonical district name

 - check_customer_status() → set urgency:

 - Active customer → "urgent" (retention risk)

 - Pipeline/prospect → "time_sensitive"

 - New → "time_sensitive"

 - compute_heat_score() with type="leadership", tier=1

 - Person name embedded in headline, not a separate field

 5. Dedup key : f"leadership_{normalized_district}_{change_type}_{year_month}" — monthly granularity prevents re-surfacing

 Cadence: Weekly auto-gate

 - Leadership scan is included in process_all_signals() and process_new_signals()

 - But gates itself: checks a module-level _last_leadership_scan timestamp. Skips if last run was <7 days ago.

 - /signal_leadership command bypasses the gate (manual override)

 - No new scheduler entry needed

 BoardDocs expansion

 - New _BOARD_LEADERSHIP_KEYWORDS regex: superintendent search, superintendent evaluation, superintendent contract, superintendent appointment, superintendent resignation, superintendent hire, superintendent

 transition

 - Matched items produce board_meeting type signals (correct — the board meeting is the signal)

 - Added to _extract_agenda_signals() alongside existing tech + bond keywords

 - Cap: 1 leadership signal per meeting (same pattern as bond cap)

 - Weekly automated: ~$0.03/scan (26 Serper + 1 Haiku)

 - BoardDocs expansion: $0 (regex only)

 - Monthly: ~$0.12

 Telegram command

 - /signal_leadership or scan leadership — manual trigger, bypasses 7-day gate

 - Output format matches other scan commands (count, top results, write confirmation)

 What this does NOT cover (Phase 2, future session)

 - Ballotpedia superintendent directory snapshots for baseline diffing

 - Small-district coverage that doesn't generate press

 - Historical baseline of current superintendents

 ---

 Does this revised design look solid, or do you want me to adjust anything before I write the spec?

 ❯ take another pass at it. You are acting as a ruthless, senior-level technical reviewer and architect. Your job is not to validate what you just produced — it's to break it, rebuild it, and hand back something

 I can execute with confidence. Do not hedge. Do not be diplomatic about problems. Be direct, specific, and opinionated.

 Before you begin: stop and think carefully about the entire plan as a whole. Don't rush into critique. Spend real effort understanding the full picture before you touch anything.

 1. Challenge the foundation. Before touching any details, ask whether the overall approach and architecture are right. Is this the best way to solve this problem, or did you default to the most obvious path? If

 there's a fundamentally better angle — a different architecture, a different sequencing, a different set of tradeoffs — say so and restructure around it. Don't anchor to your first answer just because you

 already wrote it.

 2. Stress-test every layer as if you're executing it yourself tomorrow. Walk through the plan step by step and ask: could I sit down and start building from this right now without getting stuck? Identify where

 assumptions are hiding, where steps are vague enough to stall momentum, where dependencies would create bottlenecks, and where things will quietly break at the edges. For every problem you find, name it,

 explain why it happens, explain what it costs, and provide the fix — not just the diagnosis.

 3. Validate against the best available knowledge. For each major technical decision — architecture, tooling, patterns, data modeling, sequencing — pressure-test whether your recommendation is genuinely the

 strongest option or just the most familiar. Think about what has worked well in production-grade projects, not just what's common in tutorials. If there are competing approaches with real tradeoffs, surface

 them explicitly and take a clear position on which I should use and why.

 4. Optimize for builder momentum. The plan should be structured so I can start executing immediately and maintain flow. Reorganize if needed so that early steps produce visible, tangible progress. Front-load

 decisions that unblock everything downstream. Flag anywhere the plan would force me to stop and make an ambiguous judgment call — then either make that call for me with your reasoning, or restructure so the

 decision isn't needed yet.

 5. Cut the fat, keep the muscle. Depth and complexity are welcome — but redundancy, vagueness, and ceremonial steps are not. Every element should earn its place. If something would make me pause and think "what

 does this actually mean in practice," either make it concrete or kill it. If two steps do the same work, collapse them.

 6. Fill the gaps a senior engineer would catch in a design review. What's missing that seems minor now but will cause real problems during execution? Think edge cases, error handling, sequencing issues,

 prerequisites, migration paths, and decisions that are easy to punt on but expensive to reverse later.

 7. Rewrite the full plan from scratch incorporating everything above. Do not patch the original — rebuild it with the benefit of everything you just identified. Make it comprehensive, precise, and immediately

 actionable. Then at the end, call out the most important changes you made and explain your reasoning so I can evaluate whether your thinking is sound.

 Take your time. Think deeply. Do your best work.

 ⏺ Let me take a hard look at this. Before I critique, I need to verify a few things in the codebase I'm making assumptions about.

 ⏺ Tulsa PS bond vote : It's tomorrow (April 7), not yet happened. We'll check the result then.

 ⏺ Web Search ("site:about.burbio.com superintendent turnover tracker free access")

 ⎿   Did 1 search in 5s

 ⏺ Web Search ("state education department superintendent directory public list Texas Ohio Illinois")

 ⎿   Did 1 search in 8s

 ⏺ Good research. Here's what I found:

 Tulsa PS bond vote : It's tomorrow (April 7), not yet happened. We'll check the result after polls close.

 1. Serper web search (fastest, free) — Search for "superintendent hired/resigned/retired [state]" across territory states. Parse news articles for district, person, change type. Similar to how signal

 enrichment works.

 2. BoardDocs keyword expansion (already have the scraper) — Add superintendent-specific agenda keywords to the existing 25-district scraper: "superintendent search", "superintendent appointment",

 "superintendent contract", "superintendent resignation".

 3. State education directories (baseline comparison):

 - Texas: askTED — district superintendent names

 - Ohio: OEDS — searchable directory

 - Ballotpedia — national superintendent list

 - Snapshot periodically, diff against previous to detect changes

 4. Google Alerts (already catching some) — The leadership keywords we added in Session 44 are already flowing through the existing signal pipeline.

 Part 1 (this session): scan_leadership_changes() — Serper-based web search across 13 territory states for recent superintendent moves. Programmatic parsing of search results, Claude Haiku extraction for

 structured data (name, district, change type, date). Integrates into daily scan + /signal_leadership command.

 Part 2 (this session if time): Expand BoardDocs keywords with superintendent-specific terms so existing board meeting scans catch superintendent agenda items.

 Part 3 (future): State directory snapshots for baseline comparison (more complex, per-state scrapers).

 Does this approach work? Or do you want me to adjust before building?

 ❯ enter plan mode and think carefully about your approach

 ⏺ Skill (superpowers:brainstorming)

 ⎿   Successfully loaded skill

 ⏺ I already have the signal processor architecture from the earlier exploration. Let me now dig into the specific integration points — the BoardDocs keywords, the daily scan orchestrator, and the Serper usage

 patterns.

 ⎿  Loaded tools/CLAUDE.md

 ⏺ Now let me check the Telegram command dispatch patterns for signal commands.

 ⏺ I've now read the full signal processor codebase. Here's what I understand about the integration surface:

 - Leadership is already a Tier 1 signal type (weight 30, second only to bonds at 35)

 - classify_signal() already has a leadership regex that catches superintendent + named/appointed/hired/resign/retire/interim/search/depart

 - The enrichment system already has leadership-specific Serper queries (new superintendent priorities, CTE/STEM alignment)

 - Google Alerts with leadership keywords are already set up (from Session 44)

 - No dedicated leadership scanner exists yet — the only way leadership signals currently enter is passively through Google Alerts, Burbio newsletters, or RSS feeds

 The gap is clear: there's no proactive scanner that goes looking for superintendent changes across territory states. It only catches what happens to show up in the existing email/RSS feeds.

 First clarifying question:

 What's more valuable to you — breadth or speed?

 - Option A: Broad web search scan — Use Serper to actively search for superintendent changes across all 13 territory states each day. Catches any news article mentioning superintendent moves. Broadest coverage

 but costs ~$0.01-0.02/scan (13 states × 1-2 queries each).

 - Option B: Targeted district monitoring — Focus on the ~8,100 NCES districts in territory. Periodically snapshot superintendent names from state education directories (askTED for TX, OEDS for OH, etc.) and

 diff against previous. More reliable detection but requires per-state scraper setup and is slower to build.

 - Option C: Both, phased — Start with web search (fast to build, immediate value), add directory snapshots later for completeness.

 Which direction feels right?

 ❯ Im leaning towards option optons b or c. Here's something im thinking too: for leadership changes we shouldnt be doing scanning for this everyday. more like once a week or once a month.

 ⏺ Good instincts on both counts. Superintendent changes don't happen daily — they're seasonal (most announcements cluster around Feb-June for the following school year). A weekly or monthly cadence makes much

 more sense than burning Serper credits every morning.

 Before I propose approaches, one more question:

 How do you want to act on leadership signals when they're found?

 Right now when a signal comes in, you can /signal_act N to push it to the Prospecting Queue and trigger research. For leadership changes specifically, the play is different — it's not just "new district to

 prospect," it's often:

 - A district you're already working (pipeline/prospect) where the decision-maker just changed

 - A district where the new superintendent's priorities (STEM, CTE, CS) create an opening

 - A timing window (first 90 days = most open to new initiatives)

 So should leadership signals:

 1. Just flow into the normal signal pipeline — same as bonds/BoardDocs, you review via /signals and decide what to do

 2. Get special treatment — e.g., auto-flag if the district is already in Pipeline/Active Accounts (because that's a "your champion just left" alert, not a prospecting signal)

 3. Something else you're envisioning?

 This affects whether I build just a scanner or a scanner + alerting logic.

 ❯ Im open to what you think would be best here. This one is a new one for me

 ⏺ Here's my recommendation: leadership signals should flow into the normal pipeline but with relationship-aware urgency.

 The reason is that a superintendent change means different things depending on your relationship:

 - Active customer : This is a risk signal — your champion may have just left. You need to meet the new superintendent before they review vendor contracts. Urgency = urgent.

 - Pipeline/prospect being worked : Your contact may have changed. Need to re-engage with the new leader. Urgency = time_sensitive.

 - New district (no relationship) : Classic opportunity — new superintendents in their first 90 days are most open to new programs. Urgency = time_sensitive.

 The existing customer_status field + heat score system already handles the weighting (active customers get 0.8x modifier, prospects get 0.5x). What I'd add is: if the district is an active customer, bump

 urgency to "urgent" and add a flag in the headline so it stands out in /signals as a retention risk, not just a prospecting lead.

 passive feeds miss.

 Let me now propose the approaches.

 ⏺ Three Approaches

 Approach 1: Serper Web Search Only (fast build, good coverage)

 - Weekly Serper scan: 1-2 queries per territory state ("superintendent hired OR resigned OR appointed [state] K-12 2026")

 - Claude Haiku extracts structured data from search results (district, name, change type, date)

 - ~$0.02-0.04/scan (26 Serper calls + a few Claude Haiku calls)

 - Pros : Builds in 1-2 hours, catches any published news, immediate value

 - Cons : Only catches what news outlets report. Small districts may not get press coverage. No baseline to diff against.

 Approach 2: State Directory Snapshots + Diff (reliable, comprehensive)

 - Scrape superintendent names from state education directories (askTED for TX, OEDS for OH, Ballotpedia's national list, etc.)

 - Store snapshots in a Google Sheet tab ("Leadership Baseline")

 - Monthly diff: compare current vs. previous snapshot, surface changes as signals

 - Pros : Catches every change including small districts, authoritative source, no news dependency

 - Cons : Harder to build (per-state scraper variations), some directories update slowly, initial setup is 3-5 hours. Need to handle different HTML structures per state.

 Approach 3: Web Search Now + Directory Snapshots Later (recommended)

 - Phase 1 (this session) : Serper-based scan. Weekly cadence (configurable). Relationship-aware urgency. /signal_leadership command + integration into daily scan (runs weekly, not daily). BoardDocs keyword

 expansion for superintendent agenda items.

 - Phase 2 (future session) : Ballotpedia national superintendent list as baseline snapshot (single source, covers all states). Monthly diff. Fills the small-district gap.

 - Pros : Immediate value today, comprehensive coverage later. Phase 1 is 1-2 hours, Phase 2 is a separate session.

 - Cons : Phase 1 alone misses small districts without press coverage (acceptable short-term).

 My recommendation: Approach 3. You get leadership signals flowing this session via web search. The directory snapshot is a bigger build that we tackle separately — and Ballotpedia's national list is likely the

 cleanest single source rather than scraping 13 different state directory systems.

 What do you think?

 ❯ yeah lets do approach 3

 ⏺ Design — Section 1: Scanner Architecture

 scan_leadership_changes(states=None, progress_callback=None) -> list

 Flow:

 1. For each territory state, run 1-2 Serper queries:

 - "superintendent" ("hired" OR "appointed" OR "named" OR "resigned" OR "retired" OR "interim") "[state name]" K-12 2026

 - "superintendent search" OR "superintendent vacancy" "[state name]" school district 2026

 2. Deduplicate results by URL across states

 3. Send combined search snippets to Claude Haiku in one batch call per state — extract structured JSON:

 [{ "district" : "..." , "state" : "TX" , "person_name" : "..." ,

 "change_type" : "hired|resigned|retired|interim|search" ,

 "headline" : "..." , "url" : "..." }]

 4. For each extracted signal:

 - NCES lookup to validate district name + get canonical spelling

 - check_customer_status() for relationship-aware urgency

 - If active customer → urgency = "urgent", headline prefix: " ⚠️ ACCOUNT RISK:"

 - If pipeline/prospect → urgency = "time_sensitive"

 - If new → urgency = "time_sensitive"

 - compute_heat_score() with signal_type="leadership", tier=1

 5. Dedup by message_id = f"leadership_{normalized_district}_{change_type}_{month}"

 - Monthly granularity prevents re-surfacing the same change every week

 Cost estimate : ~13 states × 2 Serper queries = 26 API calls ($0.026) + ~13 Claude Haiku calls (~$0.01) = ~$0.04/scan

 Does this structure look right so far?

 ❯ Im open for what you think is best. Just double check and look carefully at it

 ⏺ Good call — let me pressure-test my own design. I see several issues:

 Problems with my initial design:

 1. 13 separate Claude Haiku calls is wasteful. Each state's search results are ~500-1000 chars. All 13 states fit in one Haiku call easily. Should batch into 1-2 calls, not 13.

 2. Emoji prefix in headline is data pollution. " ⚠️ ACCOUNT RISK:" baked into the stored headline is messy. The risk flagging should happen in the display/formatting layer ( format_hot_signals ) based on

 customer_status + signal_type == "leadership" , not in the stored data.

 3. Cadence integration is underspecified. Steven said weekly, not daily. Two options: (a) separate weekly scheduler entry, or (b) include in the daily scan with a "last run" check that skips if run within 7

 days. Option B is cleaner — no new scheduler entry, it just gates itself.

 4. Person name has no home. The signal schema is 18 columns with no "person_name" field. Adding a column is overkill. Better: include the name in the headline ("Dr. Jane Smith appointed superintendent at

 Dallas ISD") and let enrichment pull deeper details.

 5. BoardDocs keyword expansion needs its own regex. Superintendent agenda items (search, evaluation, contract) are early-warning signals — a board discussing a superintendent search means a change is coming .

 These should be a separate _BOARD_LEADERSHIP_KEYWORDS regex, not mixed into _BOARD_TECH_KEYWORDS . They'd still produce board_meeting type signals (which is correct — the meeting is the signal, not the change

 itself).

 6. State validation on extracted results. A news article about Texas might mention a superintendent who came from Ohio. Need to validate that extracted district+state matches the searched state, or at minimum

 is in territory.

 Scanner: scan_leadership_changes()

 1. Serper queries — 2 per territory state (26 total, ~$0.026):

 - "superintendent" ("hired" OR "appointed" OR "named" OR "resigned" OR "retired") "[state name]" school district 2026

 - "superintendent" ("search" OR "vacancy" OR "interim") "[state name]" school district 2026

 2. Deduplicate URLs across all states

 3. One Claude Haiku call — send all combined snippets (~6-10K chars), extract structured JSON array. Each item: {district, state, person_name, change_type, headline, url} . Cost: ~$0.005

 4. Post-processing per signal:

 - Validate state is in territory (drop cross-references to non-territory states)

 - NCES lookup for canonical district name

 - check_customer_status() → set urgency:

 - Active customer → "urgent" (retention risk)

 - Pipeline/prospect → "time_sensitive"

 - New → "time_sensitive"

 - compute_heat_score() with type="leadership", tier=1

 - Person name embedded in headline, not a separate field

 5. Dedup key : f"leadership_{normalized_district}_{change_type}_{year_month}" — monthly granularity prevents re-surfacing

 Cadence: Weekly auto-gate

 - Leadership scan is included in process_all_signals() and process_new_signals()

 - But gates itself: checks a module-level _last_leadership_scan timestamp. Skips if last run was <7 days ago.

 - /signal_leadership command bypasses the gate (manual override)

 - No new scheduler entry needed

 BoardDocs expansion

 - New _BOARD_LEADERSHIP_KEYWORDS regex: superintendent search, superintendent evaluation, superintendent contract, superintendent appointment, superintendent resignation, superintendent hire, superintendent

 transition

 - Matched items produce board_meeting type signals (correct — the board meeting is the signal)

 - Added to _extract_agenda_signals() alongside existing tech + bond keywords

 - Cap: 1 leadership signal per meeting (same pattern as bond cap)

 - Weekly automated: ~$0.03/scan (26 Serper + 1 Haiku)

 - BoardDocs expansion: $0 (regex only)

 - Monthly: ~$0.12

 Telegram command

 - /signal_leadership or scan leadership — manual trigger, bypasses 7-day gate

 - Output format matches other scan commands (count, top results, write confirmation)

 What this does NOT cover (Phase 2, future session)

 - Ballotpedia superintendent directory snapshots for baseline diffing

 - Small-district coverage that doesn't generate press

 - Historical baseline of current superintendents

 I can execute with confidence. Do not hedge. Do not be diplomatic about problems. Be direct, specific, and opinionated.

 Before you begin: stop and think carefully about the entire plan as a whole. Don't rush into critique. Spend real effort understanding the full picture before you touch anything.

 1. Challenge the foundation. Before touching any details, ask whether the overall approach and architecture are right. Is this the best way to solve this problem, or did you default to the most obvious path? If

 there's a fundamentally better angle — a different architecture, a different sequencing, a different set of tradeoffs — say so and restructure around it. Don't anchor to your first answer just because you

 already wrote it.

 2. Stress-test every layer as if you're executing it yourself tomorrow. Walk through the plan step by step and ask: could I sit down and start building from this right now without getting stuck? Identify where

 assumptions are hiding, where steps are vague enough to stall momentum, where dependencies would create bottlenecks, and where things will quietly break at the edges. For every problem you find, name it,

 explain why it happens, explain what it costs, and provide the fix — not just the diagnosis.

 3. Validate against the best available knowledge. For each major technical decision — architecture, tooling, patterns, data modeling, sequencing — pressure-test whether your recommendation is genuinely the

 strongest option or just the most familiar. Think about what has worked well in production-grade projects, not just what's common in tutorials. If there are competing approaches with real tradeoffs, surface

 them explicitly and take a clear position on which I should use and why.

 4. Optimize for builder momentum. The plan should be structured so I can start executing immediately and maintain flow. Reorganize if needed so that early steps produce visible, tangible progress. Front-load

 decisions that unblock everything downstream. Flag anywhere the plan would force me to stop and make an ambiguous judgment call — then either make that call for me with your reasoning, or restructure so the

 decision isn't needed yet.

 5. Cut the fat, keep the muscle. Depth and complexity are welcome — but redundancy, vagueness, and ceremonial steps are not. Every element should earn its place. If something would make me pause and think "what

 does this actually mean in practice," either make it concrete or kill it. If two steps do the same work, collapse them.

 6. Fill the gaps a senior engineer would catch in a design review. What's missing that seems minor now but will cause real problems during execution? Think edge cases, error handling, sequencing issues,

 prerequisites, migration paths, and decisions that are easy to punt on but expensive to reverse later.

 7. Rewrite the full plan from scratch incorporating everything above. Do not patch the original — rebuild it with the benefit of everything you just identified. Make it comprehensive, precise, and immediately

 actionable. Then at the end, call out the most important changes you made and explain your reasoning so I can evaluate whether your thinking is sound.

 Take your time. Think deeply. Do your best work.

 ⏺ I already have the signal processor architecture from the earlier exploration. Let me now dig into the specific integration points — the BoardDocs keywords, the daily scan orchestrator, and the Serper usage

 patterns.

 ⎿  Loaded tools/CLAUDE.md

 ⏺ Now let me check the Telegram command dispatch patterns for signal commands.

 ⏺ I've now read the full signal processor codebase. Here's what I understand about the integration surface:

 - Leadership is already a Tier 1 signal type (weight 30, second only to bonds at 35)

 - classify_signal() already has a leadership regex that catches superintendent + named/appointed/hired/resign/retire/interim/search/depart

 - The enrichment system already has leadership-specific Serper queries (new superintendent priorities, CTE/STEM alignment)

 - Google Alerts with leadership keywords are already set up (from Session 44)

 - No dedicated leadership scanner exists yet — the only way leadership signals currently enter is passively through Google Alerts, Burbio newsletters, or RSS feeds

 The gap is clear: there's no proactive scanner that goes looking for superintendent changes across territory states. It only catches what happens to show up in the existing email/RSS feeds.

 First clarifying question:

 What's more valuable to you — breadth or speed?

 - Option A: Broad web search scan — Use Serper to actively search for superintendent changes across all 13 territory states each day. Catches any news article mentioning superintendent moves. Broadest coverage

 but costs ~$0.01-0.02/scan (13 states × 1-2 queries each).

 - Option B: Targeted district monitoring — Focus on the ~8,100 NCES districts in territory. Periodically snapshot superintendent names from state education directories (askTED for TX, OEDS for OH, etc.) and

 diff against previous. More reliable detection but requires per-state scraper setup and is slower to build.

 - Option C: Both, phased — Start with web search (fast to build, immediate value), add directory snapshots later for completeness.

 Which direction feels right?

 ❯ Im leaning towards option optons b or c. Here's something im thinking too: for leadership changes we shouldnt be doing scanning for this everyday. more like once a week or once a month.

 ⏺ Good instincts on both counts. Superintendent changes don't happen daily — they're seasonal (most announcements cluster around Feb-June for the following school year). A weekly or monthly cadence makes much

 more sense than burning Serper credits every morning.

 Before I propose approaches, one more question:

 How do you want to act on leadership signals when they're found?

 Right now when a signal comes in, you can /signal_act N to push it to the Prospecting Queue and trigger research. For leadership changes specifically, the play is different — it's not just "new district to

 prospect," it's often:

 - A district you're already working (pipeline/prospect) where the decision-maker just changed

 - A district where the new superintendent's priorities (STEM, CTE, CS) create an opening

 - A timing window (first 90 days = most open to new initiatives)

 So should leadership signals:

 1. Just flow into the normal signal pipeline — same as bonds/BoardDocs, you review via /signals and decide what to do

 2. Get special treatment — e.g., auto-flag if the district is already in Pipeline/Active Accounts (because that's a "your champion just left" alert, not a prospecting signal)

 3. Something else you're envisioning?

 This affects whether I build just a scanner or a scanner + alerting logic.

 ❯ Im open to what you think would be best here. This one is a new one for me

 ⏺ Here's my recommendation: leadership signals should flow into the normal pipeline but with relationship-aware urgency.

 The reason is that a superintendent change means different things depending on your relationship:

 - Active customer : This is a risk signal — your champion may have just left. You need to meet the new superintendent before they review vendor contracts. Urgency = urgent.

 - Pipeline/prospect being worked : Your contact may have changed. Need to re-engage with the new leader. Urgency = time_sensitive.

 - New district (no relationship) : Classic opportunity — new superintendents in their first 90 days are most open to new programs. Urgency = time_sensitive.

 The existing customer_status field + heat score system already handles the weighting (active customers get 0.8x modifier, prospects get 0.5x). What I'd add is: if the district is an active customer, bump

 urgency to "urgent" and add a flag in the headline so it stands out in /signals as a retention risk, not just a prospecting lead.

 passive feeds miss.

 Let me now propose the approaches.

 ⏺ Three Approaches

 Approach 1: Serper Web Search Only (fast build, good coverage)

 - Weekly Serper scan: 1-2 queries per territory state ("superintendent hired OR resigned OR appointed [state] K-12 2026")

 - Claude Haiku extracts structured data from search results (district, name, change type, date)

 - ~$0.02-0.04/scan (26 Serper calls + a few Claude Haiku calls)

 - Pros : Builds in 1-2 hours, catches any published news, immediate value

 - Cons : Only catches what news outlets report. Small districts may not get press coverage. No baseline to diff against.

 Approach 2: State Directory Snapshots + Diff (reliable, comprehensive)

 - Scrape superintendent names from state education directories (askTED for TX, OEDS for OH, Ballotpedia's national list, etc.)

 - Store snapshots in a Google Sheet tab ("Leadership Baseline")

 - Monthly diff: compare current vs. previous snapshot, surface changes as signals

 - Pros : Catches every change including small districts, authoritative source, no news dependency

 - Cons : Harder to build (per-state scraper variations), some directories update slowly, initial setup is 3-5 hours. Need to handle different HTML structures per state.

 Approach 3: Web Search Now + Directory Snapshots Later (recommended)

 - Phase 1 (this session) : Serper-based scan. Weekly cadence (configurable). Relationship-aware urgency. /signal_leadership command + integration into daily scan (runs weekly, not daily). BoardDocs keyword

 expansion for superintendent agenda items.

 - Phase 2 (future session) : Ballotpedia national superintendent list as baseline snapshot (single source, covers all states). Monthly diff. Fills the small-district gap.

 - Pros : Immediate value today, comprehensive coverage later. Phase 1 is 1-2 hours, Phase 2 is a separate session.

 - Cons : Phase 1 alone misses small districts without press coverage (acceptable short-term).

 My recommendation: Approach 3. You get leadership signals flowing this session via web search. The directory snapshot is a bigger build that we tackle separately — and Ballotpedia's national list is likely the

 cleanest single source rather than scraping 13 different state directory systems.

 What do you think?

 ❯ yeah lets do approach 3

 ⏺ Design — Section 1: Scanner Architecture

 scan_leadership_changes(states=None, progress_callback=None) -> list

 Flow:

 1. For each territory state, run 1-2 Serper queries:

 - "superintendent" ("hired" OR "appointed" OR "named" OR "resigned" OR "retired" OR "interim") "[state name]" K-12 2026

 - "superintendent search" OR "superintendent vacancy" "[state name]" school district 2026

 2. Deduplicate results by URL across states

 3. Send combined search snippets to Claude Haiku in one batch call per state — extract structured JSON:

 [{ "district" : "..." , "state" : "TX" , "person_name" : "..." ,

 "change_type" : "hired|resigned|retired|interim|search" ,

 "headline" : "..." , "url" : "..." }]

 4. For each extracted signal:

 - NCES lookup to validate district name + get canonical spelling

 - check_customer_status() for relationship-aware urgency

 - If active customer → urgency = "urgent", headline prefix: " ⚠️ ACCOUNT RISK:"

 - If pipeline/prospect → urgency = "time_sensitive"

 - If new → urgency = "time_sensitive"

 - compute_heat_score() with signal_type="leadership", tier=1

 5. Dedup by message_id = f"leadership_{normalized_district}_{change_type}_{month}"

 - Monthly granularity prevents re-surfacing the same change every week

 Cost estimate : ~13 states × 2 Serper queries = 26 API calls ($0.026) + ~13 Claude Haiku calls (~$0.01) = ~$0.04/scan

 ❯ Im open for what you think is best. Just double check and look carefully at it

 3. Cadence integration is underspecified. Steven said weekly, not daily. Two options: (a) separate weekly scheduler entry, or (b) include in the daily scan with a "last run" check that skips if run within 7

 4. Person name has no home. The signal schema is 18 columns with no "person_name" field. Adding a column is overkill. Better: include the name in the headline ("Dr. Jane Smith appointed superintendent at

 5. BoardDocs keyword expansion needs its own regex. Superintendent agenda items (search, evaluation, contract) are early-warning signals — a board discussing a superintendent search means a change is coming .

 These should be a separate _BOARD_LEADERSHIP_KEYWORDS regex, not mixed into _BOARD_TECH_KEYWORDS . They'd still produce board_meeting type signals (which is correct — the meeting is the signal, not the change

 itself).

 6. State validation on extracted results. A news article about Texas might mention a superintendent who came from Ohio. Need to validate that extracted district+state matches the searched state, or at minimum

 is in territory.

 5. Cut the fat, keep the muscle. Depth and complexity are welcome — but redundancy, vagueness, and ceremonial steps are not. Every element should earn its place. If something would make me pause and think "what

 does this actually mean in practice," either make it concrete or kill it. If two steps do the same work, collapse them.

 6. Fill the gaps a senior engineer would catch in a design review. What's missing that seems minor now but will cause real problems during execution? Think edge cases, error handling, sequencing issues,

 prerequisites, migration paths, and decisions that are easy to punt on but expensive to reverse later.

 7. Rewrite the full plan from scratch incorporating everything above. Do not patch the original — rebuild it with the benefit of everything you just identified. Make it comprehensive, precise, and immediately

 actionable. Then at the end, call out the most important changes you made and explain your reasoning so I can evaluate whether your thinking is sound.

 Take your time. Think deeply. Do your best work.

 ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌

 Leadership Change Monitoring — Implementation Plan

 Context

 Superintendent turnovers are a Tier 1 buying signal for K-12 ed-tech sales. New superintendents are most open to new programs in their first 90 days. If an existing customer's superintendent leaves, that's a

 retention risk. Currently, Scout only catches leadership changes passively through Google Alerts, Burbio newsletters, or RSS feeds. We need proactive weekly scanning.

 Phase 1 (this session): Serper web search scanner + BoardDocs keyword expansion + weekly scheduler + Telegram command.

 Phase 2 (future session): Ballotpedia superintendent directory snapshot + monthly diff for comprehensive coverage.

 Cost: ~$0.03/scan, running weekly = ~$0.12/month.

 ---

 Files to Modify

 1. tools/signal_processor.py — core scanner function, BoardDocs keywords, display risk flagging

 2. agent/scheduler.py — weekly Monday event

 3. agent/main.py — command handler + scheduler wiring

 4. tools/CLAUDE.md + agent/CLAUDE.md — API docs

 ---

 Step 1: Add _BOARD_LEADERSHIP_KEYWORDS regex

 File: tools/signal_processor.py , after line 1554 (end of _BOARD_BOND_KEYWORDS ), before _BOARDDOCS_HEADERS at line 1556.

 _BOARD_LEADERSHIP_KEYWORDS = re. compile (

 r"\b(?:superintendent.(?:search|resignation|retirement|appointment|hire|vacancy|transition)|"

 r"interim.superintendent)\b" ,

 re.IGNORECASE,

 )

 Excluded keywords (noise): evaluation, contract, report, update — routine annual items.

 ---

 Step 2: Add leadership block in _extract_agenda_signals()

 File: tools/signal_processor.py , after the bond signal block (line 1721), before return signals (line 1723).

 Pattern mirrors the bond block (lines 1698-1721). Cap at 1 leadership signal per meeting.

 # Emit at most 1 leadership signal per meeting

 leadership_match = _BOARD_LEADERSHIP_KEYWORDS.search(agenda_text)

 if leadership_match:

 ldr_keyword = leadership_match.group( 0 ).strip()

 ldr_trailing = agenda_text[leadership_match.end():leadership_match.end() + 150 ].strip()

 ldr_trailing = re.sub( r"^\W+" , "" , ldr_trailing)

 heat = compute_heat_score( "board_meeting" , 1 , in_territory, cust_status)

 headline = f"Board: {ldr_keyword} — {ldr_trailing[: 120 ]}"

 signals.append({

 "date" : meeting_date,

 "source" : "boarddocs" ,

 "source_detail" : f"BoardDocs — {district_name}" ,

 "signal_type" : "board_meeting" ,

 "scope" : "district" ,

 "district" : district_name,

 "state" : state,

 "headline" : headline,

 "dollar_amount" : "" ,

 "tier" : 1 ,

 "heat_score" : heat,

 "urgency" : "time_sensitive" ,

 "customer_status" : cust_status,

 "url" : meeting_url,

 "message_id" : f"bd_{org_code}_{meeting_id}_leadership" ,

 })

 signal_type = "board_meeting" is intentional — the board meeting discussing a superintendent change is the signal, not the change itself.

 ---

 Step 3: Add scan_leadership_changes() function

 File: tools/signal_processor.py , after scan_ballotpedia() (ends line 1937), before the RSS section (line 1940).

 Function: scan_leadership_changes(states=None, progress_callback=None) -> list

 Flow:

 1. Guard: if not SERPER_API_KEY , log warning, return []

 2. import httpx (lazy, same as line 1173)

 3. states = states or list(TERRITORY_STATES) (no CA — too noisy for superintendent news, same pattern as Ballotpedia)

 4. For each state, get full name via ABBR_TO_STATE_NAME (line 161)

 5. Run 2 Serper queries per state:

 - "superintendent" ("hired" OR "appointed" OR "named" OR "resigned" OR "retired") "{state_name}" school district

 - "superintendent" ("search" OR "vacancy" OR "interim") "{state_name}" school district

 - POST to SERPER_URL with json={"q": query, "num": 10, "tbs": "qdr:m"} (past month, not past week — superintendent news can take time to surface; weekly scan with monthly lookback catches things that

 appeared mid-week)

 - time.sleep(0.5) between calls

 - Collect into all_snippets list, dedup by URL via seen_urls set

 - Each entry: {"title": ..., "snippet": ..., "url": ..., "searched_state": state_abbr}

 - try/except per POST, continue on failure

 6. If no results, return []

 7. Build combined text from all snippets (~6-10K chars)

 8. Single Claude Haiku call ( claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 , max_tokens=2000, timeout=90.0):

 - Prompt: extract K-12 school district superintendent changes ONLY. Exclude police/public works/university. Return JSON array: [{district, state, person_name, change_type, headline}]

 - change_type: one of hired, appointed, resigned, retired, interim, search

 - _track_usage(response) for cost tracking

 9. Parse JSON: strip markdown fences, find [ / ] boundaries, json.loads() , try/except returns []

 10. Post-process each item:

 - Validate state is in TERRITORY_STATES_WITH_CA — skip if not

 - NCES lookup via lookup_district_state(district) for validation/correction

 - check_customer_status(district) for relationship status

 - Urgency: active customer → "urgent", everything else → "time_sensitive"

 - compute_heat_score("leadership", 1, in_territory=True, cust_status)

 - Headline: use Claude's headline, which includes person name

 - Normalize district via csv_importer.normalize_name(district)

 - message_id = f"leadership_{norm_district}_{change_type}_{datetime.now().strftime('%Y_%m')}"

 - url = "" — critical for dedup. write_signals composite key msg_id|url falls back to just msg_id when url is empty. Store article domain in source_detail.

 11. Dedup within scan by (norm_district, change_type) — keep first occurrence

 12. Return signal list

 Signal dict shape:

 {

 "date" : "2026-04-07" ,

 "source" : "leadership_scan" ,

 "source_detail" : f"Serper scan — {urlparse(best_url).netloc}" ,

 "signal_type" : "leadership" ,

 "scope" : "district" ,

 "district" : district,

 "state" : state,

 "headline" : "Dr. Jane Smith appointed superintendent at Dallas ISD" ,

 "dollar_amount" : "" ,

 "tier" : 1 ,

 "heat_score" : heat,

 "urgency" : "urgent" or "time_sensitive" ,

 "customer_status" : cust_status,

 "url" : "" ,

 "message_id" : "leadership_dallas_isd_appointed_2026_04" ,

 }

 File: agent/scheduler.py

 self._last_leadership_scan_date = None

 ---

 Step 6: Telegram command + scheduler wiring

 File: agent/main.py

 /signal_leadership command handler (after /signal_board handler, ~line 2813):

 Aliases: /signal_leadership , signal leadership , scan leadership

 elif user_text.lower() in [ "/signal_leadership" , "signal leadership" , "scan leadership" ]:

 await send_message( "👔 Scanning for superintendent changes..." )

 try :

 loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()

 leadership_signals = await loop.run_in_executor(

 None , signal_processor.scan_leadership_changes, None , None )

 if leadership_signals:

 write_result = await loop.run_in_executor(

 None , signal_processor.write_signals, leadership_signals)

 lines = [ f"👔 *Leadership Scan Complete* — { len (leadership_signals)} changes found\n" ]

 for i, sig in enumerate (leadership_signals[: 10 ], 1 ):

 dist = sig.get( "district" , "" )

 state = sig.get( "state" , "" )

 headline = sig.get( "headline" , "" )[: 80 ]

 cust = sig.get( "customer_status" , "" )

 risk = " ⚠️ " if cust == "active" else ""

 lines.append( f" {risk}{i}. {dist} ({state}) — {headline}" )

 if len (leadership_signals) > 10 :

 lines.append( f"\n ... and { len (leadership_signals) - 10 } more" )

 lines.append( f"\nWritten: {write_result['written']} | Deduped: {write_result['skipped']}" )

 await send_message( "\n" .join(lines))

 else :

 await send_message( "No superintendent changes found." )

 except Exception as e:

 await send_message( f"Leadership scan failed: {e}" )

 return

 _run_leadership_scan() async function (after _run_daily_signal_scan , ~line 3119):

 async def _run_leadership_scan():

 """Scheduled weekly leadership scan — Mondays at 8:00 AM CST."""

 try :

 loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()

 signals = await loop.run_in_executor(

 None , signal_processor.scan_leadership_changes, None , None )

 if signals:

 write_result = await loop.run_in_executor(

 None , signal_processor.write_signals, signals)

 lines = [ f"👔 *Weekly Leadership Scan* — { len (signals)} changes found\n" ]

 for i, sig in enumerate (signals[: 10 ], 1 ):

 dist = sig.get( "district" , "" )

 state = sig.get( "state" , "" )

 headline = sig.get( "headline" , "" )[: 80 ]

 cust = sig.get( "customer_status" , "" )

 risk = " ⚠️ " if cust == "active" else ""

 lines.append( f" {risk}{i}. {dist} ({state}) — {headline}" )

 lines.append( f"\nWritten: {write_result['written']} | Deduped: {write_result['skipped']}" )

 await send_message( "\n" .join(lines))

 else :

 logger.info( "Weekly leadership scan: no changes found" )

 except Exception as e:

 logger.error( f"Weekly leadership scan failed: {e}" )

 Scheduler event handler (after line 3447):

 elif sched_event == "leadership_scan" :

 asyncio.create_task(_run_leadership_scan())

 Verification

 1. Unit test the scanner locally: Run python -c "from tools.signal_processor import scan_leadership_changes; print(scan_leadership_changes())" from the repo root (with SERPER_API_KEY set). Should return a list

 of signal dicts for territory states.

 2. Test BoardDocs keywords: Run /signal_board from Telegram after deploying. Check if any superintendent-related agenda items surface as new signals.

 3. Test Telegram command: Send /signal_leadership to Scout. Should show results or "No superintendent changes found."

 4. Test dedup: Run /signal_leadership twice. Second run should show 0 written (all deduped).

 5. Test display risk flagging: If any found signal has customer_status="active", verify the ⚠️ RISK prefix appears in the output.

 6. Test weekly scheduler: Check scheduler.py logic: on a Monday at 8:00 AM, check() should return "leadership_scan". On Tuesday at 8:00 AM, should return None.

 7. Deploy to Railway: git push , wait for "Scout is online" in Telegram, then test /signal_leadership .

 Key Design Decisions

 ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

 │ Decision │ Why │

 ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ url="" in signal dict │ write_signals dedup key is msg_id|url . Empty url → key = just msg_id → same change from different news sources deduplicates correctly │

 ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Serper tbs: "qdr:m" (past month) │ Weekly scan with monthly lookback catches news that surfaced mid-cycle. Past-week would miss articles published right after previous scan │

 ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ TERRITORY_STATES not TERRITORY_STATES_WITH_CA │ CA has too many superintendent changes to be useful (same pattern as Ballotpedia bond scan) │

 ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ signal_type="board_meeting" for BoardDocs superintendent items │ The board meeting is the signal. The actual leadership change flows through the Serper scanner │

 ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Separate weekly scheduler event (not gated in daily scan) │ Cleaner than module-level timestamp gate. No hidden state. Explicit cadence. Worst-case double-fire on Monday redeploy is harmless │

 ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ 1 Claude Haiku call for all states (not 13) │ ~6-10K chars total fits easily. Saves cost and latency │

 ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

 │ Active customer → urgency="urgent" │ Retention risk outranks prospecting. Display-layer adds ⚠️ prefix so it stands out │

 └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

 ╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌╌
